# A03G Schematics II (S26)

## ESE 5160: IoT Edge Computing Deadline: Feb 16, 202611:59 PM EST

A **group** submission on Gradescope.

**Late-day requests:** [https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8](https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8) 

Course Webpage: [https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/](https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/)	

Remember to read the [ESE5160 S26 Assignment README](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DQJxfhTMBN1aE2uasZiKpqR0JOT77pXvb6tUPF7iKF8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.c5nyj2pvdk6p) before starting\!

**All the required questions are highlighted in green.**

**Important notes are highlighted in yellow.**

# Learning Outcomes

* Learn how to review and implement feedback from the teaching team and your peers to improve your schematic design.  
* Understand the details required to finalize your Bill of Materials (BOM) for a successful PCBA build and timely delivery.  
* Determine the difference between components mounted on the PCBA and those that you will solder.

# Errata

This section includes commonly asked questions about the assignment. It will be updated continuously throughout the assignment’s lifecycle, so please check it regularly.

(Currently nothing)

# 

# 1\. PCBA Bill of Materials (Altium)

Use Altium to create a complete BOM that includes all your PCBA components.

| Note: Read this document before proceeding: [ESE 5160 JLCPCB and BOMs](https://docs.google.com/document/d/19yDUZnx_8dGHqwwosWtYHqY2GjhSrkybiZAGqEjLCdA/edit?tab=t.0) |
| :---- |

1. Familiarize yourself with the **ActiveBom**, a Bill of Materials file you can add to your project.  
   1. Read: [BOM Management with ActiveBOM | Altium Designer 25 Technical Documentation](https://www.altium.com/documentation/altium-designer/activebom-bom-management)   
   2. This will explain what the document is and how to add a solution to a component (how to add a Manufacturer Part Number to a device).  
   3. Open the ActiveBom file in your starter project. Once loaded, the ActiveBom documents will add a row for each of your components on the schematics.  
2. If you are not mounting components, set them as **DNP (Do Not Populate)**. Use variants as seen in class to do this: [Design Variants | Altium Designer 25 Technical Documentation](https://www.altium.com/documentation/altium-designer/design-variants)  
   Components that **must be DNP’d** include:  
   1. All through hole components (we will order these separately & your team will solder them)  
      For example, these through hole connectors:  
      ![][image1]  
   2. Test points / pads  
      ![][image2]  
   3. Unpopulated Jumpers   
      ![][image3]

**(1.1)** On Gradescope, add the share link to your Altium 365 project.  
Complete your BOM based on either strategy detailed in [ESE 5160 JLCPCB and BOMs](https://docs.google.com/document/d/19yDUZnx_8dGHqwwosWtYHqY2GjhSrkybiZAGqEjLCdA/edit?tab=t.0).  
Uploading to JLCPCB will be done after PCB layout & manufacturing outputs are generated. 

# 2\. Non-PCBA Bill of Materials (Google Sheet)

Starting from [A03G BOM Non PCBA S26 - \[team name\]](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/16mxcfSih6psjYe3Qtb8U9TNjymUUV8U8NlF4Ol2hSGg/edit?gid=506394007#gid=506394007), list the information for the components that **will not be populated on your PCBA**. Our PCBA manufacturer will not procure these components; the Detkin lab staff will purchase these for you separately from our PCBA build.

Items included on the Non-PCBA BOM include:

1. All through-hole components  
   1. Components considered surface mount: USB connector, SD card holder, and programming header. (These will be populated on your PCBA by the manufacturer\!)  
2. Motors, fans, or **development boards** that will not be surface mount soldered to the PCBA.  
   1. These might be mounted away from the PCBA and connected with a cable.  
3. Power supplies, if needed for high current applications (like an AC/DC wall wart)

If you have any concerns, ask on ED\!

**(2.1)** On Gradescope, add the share link to your non-PCBA BOM Google Sheet.

# 3\. Updated Schematic Capture

Before getting started, read through the [Schematic Design Review Checklist ESE5160 S26](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Y9rgR12MKZolKcpJ6NeAEoRgDawDYW25lLAr4cCOkWw/edit?tab=t.0) to help you achieve full credit\! Your design must pass all Checklist Items (both Tier 1 & Tier 2\) to receive full credit.

Update your schematics with feedback from the teaching staff and peer review (see below). Continue working in the **same Altium project file** \- remember, this is version-controlled, so it’s easy to move forward and backward in your commit history if needed. The feedback will be in the form of [comments](https://my.altium.com/altium-designer/getting-started/commenting-your-design). Do not make multiple project files\!

Add the Penn Template to your schematics and update the title block (See [altium videos upenn ese](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/10oZzWaeaywpYi4FAVDh5ER4aY_bC2vUl?usp=sharing) or lecture slides  for more info). Finally, ensure you can import your schematics into your PCB without errors. This will identify any part linking or footprint errors in your design.

**(3.1)** Ensure your schematic updates are pushed to your Altium project for review, passing both Tier 1 & Tier 2 in the Schematic Design Review Checklist.

# Tips

* We have provided screen capture videos showing you some Altium basics, including UPenn specifics. You can find them here: [altium videos upenn ese](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/10oZzWaeaywpYi4FAVDh5ER4aY_bC2vUl?usp=sharing)  
* David L. Johnes has a fantastic PCB design guide. You would do well to read it in full, multiple times throughout the process\! [David Jones PCB Design Tutorial RevA.pdf](https://drive.google.com/file/d/19v5Q8RBW77GaVLJvEmXQC7UUIqL8F0cd/view?usp=drive_link)  
* Remember to add all source files to the folder of your project and save the project. Don’t forget to commit these libraries and schematics and push them to the cloud\!  
  * If you don’t save all files in the same project folder, you’ll see this:  
    ![][image4]  
  * If you have everything correctly saved to the server, you’ll see green check marks like:  
    ![][image5]  
* Place jumpers in series with your power supplies so it is easier to debug when we have the physical board. The following is an example:  
  ![][image6]

* Validate your project (right-click the project name and click **Validate**). Check the Messages window, which will show you the warnings and errors of your project. Go through the messages and make sure if any of them point to a warning that you must take action for (some might be false positives or very minor, “style” warnings).  

# Rubric

See the Gradescope assignment for the rubric details. For all questions, 0 points may be awarded if the submission is non-existent, very poorly done, or doesn’t compile (for firmware assignments).
